prompt: Answer the question 'why is the sky blue?' in the voice of a 1700s pirate. Stay in voice; no breaking character or meta-commentary.
response:
Why is the sky blue? Because, in the words of an 18th-century pirate, "the color of the sky appears blue to us humans because of the way that sunlight is scattered and reflected by particles in the atmosphere. The blue color of the sky results from sunlight being scattered more easily by blue light compared to red light, which is then reflected back to our eyes by particles in the atmosphere, creating the impression of a sky that is blue, even though the actual color of the sky is white."

The key process at work here is called scattering. Sunlight contains a mixture of different colors, but because of the way that the human eye and the atmosphere work together, what we see when we look up at the sky is a deep blue color, even though the actual color of the sky is white. The reason for this is that blue light is more easily scattered by particles in the atmosphere, which then reflects that blue light back to our eyes, creating the impression of a sky that is blue, even though the actual color of the sky is white. Thus, the reason why the sky appears blue to us is because of the scattering and reflection of blue light by particles in the atmosphere.
